finding: a property of static stimuli that implies a process involving real stimuli produced adaptation patterns matching the real process
an evolutionarily relevant threat category showed enhanced detection rates compared to non-threatening categories in both young children and adults
a spatial-distance prime, compared to spatial-closeness prime, reduced emotional and evaluative responses across multiple judgment domains

self-report and peer-report measures of a trait did not correlate with behavioral predictors of that trait, but perceptions of national character did, suggesting the former lack validity as cross-national markers
instrumental goals can override short-term hedonic goals; individuals sought an unpleasant emotion when it was instrumentally useful for an anticipated task
people believe optimistically biased personal predictions are normatively appropriate, suggesting optimistic bias reflects values rather than pure irrationality
fairness increased happiness ratings and reward-region activation independent of monetary value; tolerating unfair treatment for material gain involved suppression of negative affect
gender differences in misperceiving a signal stemmed from perceptual insensitivity rather than from differential decision thresholds
a trait was associated with systematically faster spatial attention along a vertical dimension, supporting metaphorical representation in online processing
happiness was independently predicted by having desired items and wanting possessed items; the latter partially mediated effects of gratitude and maximization
attention withdrawal degraded local feature representation more than summary-statistic representation, suggesting information outside focal attention is encoded abstractly
in intergroup competition, contributions were made to in-group cooperation rather than out-group harm; preplay communication increased in-group cooperation without increasing intergroup competition
a crowd average reduced error below individual averages when individuals' errors were statistically independent
sleep selectively preserved memory for emotional components of scenes while forgetting background details; awake time led to equivalent forgetting of both
temporally equivalent events were valued more highly when positioned in the future than in the past; greater affect toward future events mediated this asymmetry
activating the concept of physical purity or physically cleansing after disgust reduced the severity of moral judgments, reversing the typical disgust-induced moral amplification
holding heavy versus light objects increased importance judgments and elaboration of thinking, suggesting abstract concepts are grounded in bodily weight experience
asymmetries in cortical body maps predicted asymmetries in conscious body-size perception; this relationship was absent in individuals with symmetrical cortical representations
moral judgments were consistent with a principle while most subjects could not consciously justify the principle, indicating the principle operates outside conscious reasoning
the valence of a side-effect influenced knowledge attributions; agents were less likely attributed knowledge of good side-effects than bad side-effects
